preFix.title: Clamping and re-gluing a split rail
preFix.subtitle: Clean the old glue joint, apply fresh hide or PVA glue, cramp square, and allow to cure correctly
preFix.excerpt: How to re-glue a split or loose rail on a chair or table. Covers cleaning the old glue from the joint surfaces, choosing between hide glue and PVA, clamping square without bruising the timber, and curing time.
preFix.sourceNotes: Traditional UK cabinet-making and furniture restoration practice.

content.text: Re-gluing a loose or split rail works reliably if two conditions are met. First, all old glue must come off both surfaces before the new glue goes on. Second, the joint must stay in cramps without movement until the glue sets. Any dried glue left on the joint face acts as a spacer and stops the new adhesive bonding timber to timber.
Step 1, Dismantle the joint and clean the surfaces
If the rail has split along a glue line, the two surfaces separate. If the joint is loose but held by old glue, apply steam or a hot-damp rag to the joint for 3 to 5 minutes to soften

, it releases cleanly with gentle separation. Scrape both joint faces with a sharp chisel held flat to the surface until all old glue is gone and bare timber is visible. A light sand with 120-grit on the flat face (not a curved tenon, do not remove material there) removes any last residue. Both faces must feel dry and clean.

For antique furniture with original hide-glue joints, use fresh hide glue, it bonds directly to the cleaned hide-glue surface. Apply hot hide glue (60 to 65°C) to both faces with a small brush, working quickly as it gels in under 60 seconds on cold wood. For modern or unknown glue joints, PVA is suitable, apply to both faces, allow to tack for 2 minutes, then assemble. A thin even coat is the target: a squeeze-out bead along the joint line when cramped is correct; no squeeze-out means insufficient glue.

Apply the sash cramp across the joint with softwood pads between the cramp heads and the timber, direct metal-on-timber contact bruises the surface. Tighten until the glue squeeze-out appears along the full joint length, then stop, overtightening a mortise-and-tenon joint crushes the tenon shoulder and opens a gap at the face. Check

: measure diagonals with a tape. Adjust the cramp angle (a sash cramp angled away from the longer diagonal pulls it shorter) until diagonals are equal. Leave in cramps for 4 hours for PVA, or 30 minutes for hide glue (hide glue reaches full strength faster).

After the initial set (30 minutes), remove any squeeze-out with a sharp chisel while it is still slightly rubbery, cured glue is much harder to remove and can tear the wood surface. After full cure, sand any remaining glue residue flush with 120-grit. Match the existing finish, oil, wax, or shellac, over the repair area.
